title: Messy CSV Parser Worked Example
goal: Hand-roll a parser for a messy vendor CSV dialect, using objective sample tests to settle an architecture bake-off and a single deep-critic judge for maintainability.

scorers.note: Correctness suite over committed samples; the must-pass core samples gate the champion.
Every malformed input must raise ParseError.
A solution that imports the stdlib csv module fails the gate.

tracks.label: Line-based regex track
tracks.hypothesis: A per-line regex tokeniser can handle quoting within a line.

tracks.label: Character state-machine track
tracks.hypothesis: A character scanner can cross line boundaries and absorb the whole dialect.

tracks.label: Synthesis
tracks.hypothesis: Merge the hardened state machine with the regex track's concise finalisation.

samples.description: A trailing newline adds no empty row.
A comma inside a quoted field is data, not a delimiter.
A newline inside quotes stays in the field (one row).
A doubled "" is one literal quote.
Realistic vendor block: quoted commas, an embedded newline, and doubled quotes together.
A quoted empty field is an empty string.
A leading UTF-8 BOM is stripped.
Blank lines between records are skipped.
Whitespace around unquoted fields is trimmed.

iterations.hypothesis: Splitting each line on commas is the simplest thing that could possibly work.

commands.build: Emit the next parser variant from run_example.py.
commands.judge: Read test-results.json; gate on must-pass core samples; read judge.md.

scores.notes: The baseline establishes the bar. It is tiny and readable, but it treats every comma as a delimiter and every quote as literal text, so it fails all four must-pass core samples. It never raises on malformed input because it does not understand quoting at all.

lesson.trigger: Splitting each line on commas is the simplest thing that could possibly work.
lesson.action: Handle quotes so commas and quotes inside a field stop breaking rows.

iterations.hypothesis: A per-line regex that understands quotes can handle embedded commas and doubled quotes.

commands.build: Emit the next parser variant from run_example.py.
commands.judge: Read test-results.json; gate on must-pass core samples; read judge.md.

scores.notes: This is so close — 13 of 15 samples pass, including quoted commas, doubled quotes, the BOM and the dialect niceties. But it splits the input into lines before parsing, so a quoted field containing a newline is torn in half. The quoted_newline and vendor_multiline core samples fail, and no amount of regex tuning fixes it: the architecture is line-based. The objective test settles the debate — reject the track.

lesson.trigger: A per-line regex that understands quotes can handle embedded commas and doubled quotes.
lesson.action: Abandon the line-based approach: it cannot represent a field that spans two lines.

iterations.hypothesis: Scanning character by character with a quote state can cross line boundaries safely.

commands.build: Emit the next parser variant from run_example.py.
commands.judge: Read test-results.json; gate on must-pass core samples; read judge.md.

scores.notes: Switching architecture pays off immediately: the state machine passes every must-pass core sample, including the embedded newline that killed the regex track. It is a clean, readable loop. It does not yet strip the BOM, skip comments or blank lines, or trim unquoted whitespace, and it is lenient rather than raising on malformed input — but the hard part is done and the approach is sound.

lesson.trigger: Scanning character by character with a quote state can cross line boundaries safely.
lesson.action: Add the vendor-dialect niceties (BOM, comments, blank lines, trimming) and clean errors.

iterations.hypothesis: The state machine can absorb the dialect rules and report malformed input cleanly.

commands.build: Emit the next parser variant from run_example.py.
commands.judge: Read test-results.json; gate on must-pass core samples; read judge.md.

scores.notes: Now every sample passes and every malformed input raises a ParseError that names the line and column. Correctness and robustness are both at 100%. The trade-off is a busier function: four field-finalisation sites repeat the strip-if-unquoted rule, which is where the next loop can improve maintainability.

lesson.trigger: The state machine can absorb the dialect rules and report malformed input cleanly.
lesson.action: Tidy the field-finalisation logic without regressing correctness or robustness.

iterations.hypothesis: Merge the hardened state machine with the regex track's concise field-finalisation helper.

commands.build: Emit the next parser variant from run_example.py.
commands.judge: Read test-results.json; gate on must-pass core samples; read judge.md.

scores.notes: Synthesis keeps the hardened state machine's behaviour exactly — identical results on all 15 samples and all 3 malformed inputs — but folds the repeated strip-if-unquoted logic into a single finalize() helper borrowed from the regex track's field handling. Correctness and robustness hold at 100% while maintainability improves, so it takes the champion on the weighted scorecard even though the objective scores are tied with the hardened loop.

lesson.trigger: Merge the hardened state machine with the regex track's concise field-finalisation helper.
lesson.action: Stop: correctness and robustness are saturated and the code is at its clearest.

iterations.stop_reason: Correctness and robustness saturated at 100%; synthesis is the clearest champion.

best.why: Only variant that passes all 15 samples and all 3 malformed inputs with the clearest code; wins the weighted scorecard.

rules.trigger: The correctness suite has must-pass core samples (embedded commas, embedded newlines, doubled-quote escaping).
rules.action: Treat them as a hard gate: a variant that fails a core sample cannot become champion, and if its architecture can never pass one, reject the track.

rules.trigger: A candidate ties the champion's top correctness score.
rules.action: Break the tie with the weighted scorecard (robustness plus judged clarity and maintainability), not correctness alone.

rules.trigger: The vendor dialect forbids the stdlib csv module.
rules.action: Run the constraint gate on every candidate; importing csv fails regardless of other scores.

synthesis: The line-based regex track parsed 13 of 15 samples, but an objective test — a quoted field spanning two lines — proved it structurally cannot represent embedded newlines, so it was rejected. The character state machine passed every core sample, hardening added the vendor-dialect niceties and clean ParseError reporting, and synthesis merged that hardened core with the regex track's concise field-finalisation helper: correctness and robustness stay at 100% while maintainability improves to take the champion.
